patternName: TDD London School with React Hooks Behavioral Contracts

overview.description: Highly effective application of TDD London School methodology to React hooks development with behavioral contracts and interface-first design
overview.applicability: React frontend development with complex state management and external integrations

mockingStrategy.principle: Mock external dependencies, test behavior not implementation
application: localStorage operations mocked for command history testing
clipboard API mocked for copy/export functionality
file download operations mocked for export testing
React event objects mocked for keyboard interaction testing
benefits: Fast test execution without I/O dependencies
Reliable tests independent of browser capabilities
Clear separation between unit and integration concerns

behavioralContracts.principle: Define what the code should do, not how it should do it

behaviors: addCommand() must store commands and filter duplicates
getPreviousCommand() must navigate backward through history
getNextCommand() must navigate forward through history
clearHistory() must remove all stored commands
saveToStorage() must persist commands to localStorage
loadFromStorage() must restore commands from localStorage
copyMessage() must copy individual message content to clipboard
copyAllOutput() must copy complete session output
exportToText() must format content as plain text
exportToJSON() must format content as structured JSON
exportToMarkdown() must format content with markdown syntax
downloadFile() must trigger file download with correct MIME type

interfaceFirstDesign.principle: Design interfaces before implementation to ensure testability
process: Define TypeScript interfaces with behavioral contracts
Write behavioral tests against interface contracts
Implement real classes following interface specifications
advantages: Clear API contracts prevent integration issues

hookPattern.structure: Custom hook wrapping behavioral contract implementation
benefits: React state management integrated with business logic
Clean separation between UI and business concerns

step1_contractDefinition.description: Define TypeScript interface with all required behaviors

step2_classImplementation.description: Implement interface with concrete class handling business logic
step2_classImplementation.validation: Class passes all behavioral contract tests

step3_hookWrapper.description: Create custom hook that manages React state and wraps implementation
step3_hookWrapper.validation: Hook provides clean API for React components

step4_componentIntegration.description: Integrate hook into React components with proper event handling
step4_componentIntegration.example: handleInputKeyDown integration with arrow key navigation
step4_componentIntegration.validation: Full user interaction workflow tested and working

unitTests.mockingApproach: Mock all external dependencies (localStorage, clipboard, DOM)
unitTests.validationFocus: Behavior correctness and edge case handling
testCases: File export with multiple format support

integrationTests.scope: Hook integration with React component lifecycle
integrationTests.mockingApproach: Mock external services, test React integration points
testCases: Hook state updates triggering component re-renders
Event handler integration with keyboard navigation
Performance optimization with React.memo and useCallback

storageContract.behavior: Commands must persist across browser sessions
storageContract.testScenario: Add commands, reload hook, verify persistence

navigationContract.behavior: Arrow keys must navigate through command history
navigationContract.testScenario: Simulate arrow key presses, verify command retrieval
edgeCases: Mixed arrow key and typing interaction

clipboardContract.behavior: Content must copy to clipboard with proper formatting
clipboardContract.testScenario: Mock clipboard API, verify content and format

exportContract.behavior: Files must download with correct format and content
exportContract.testScenario: Mock file download, verify content and MIME type

localStorageMocking.pattern: Create fake localStorage with get/set/remove methods

fileMocking.pattern: Mock DOM createElement and URL APIs for file operations

stateAssertions.pattern: Verify state changes match expected behavior

interactionTesting.pattern: Simulate user interactions and verify responses

integrationStrategy.principle: Add features without modifying existing functionality
techniques: Event handler composition instead of replacement
UI component addition without layout changes

coverage: No performance degradation in existing features

implementationQuality.performanceOptimization: React hooks optimized with proper dependencies

integrationSuccess.deploymentRisk: Minimal - features can be safely toggled
integrationSuccess.rollbackCapability: Full rollback possible without data loss

adaptationStrategies: Adjust mock complexity based on external dependencies
Scale behavioral contract granularity for domain complexity
Customize hook patterns for specific React patterns
Tailor test scenarios for different user interaction models
Modify integration approach for different component architectures
commonPitfalls: Over-mocking leading to implementation details in tests
Insufficient behavioral coverage allowing bugs to escape
Tight coupling between tests and implementation
Inadequate error handling for edge cases
Poor integration strategy causing regression risks

positivePatterns: Interface-first design with comprehensive behavioral contracts
Proper mocking of external dependencies for fast, reliable tests
React hooks integration maintaining clean separation of concerns
Comprehensive edge case coverage with error handling
negativePatterns: Implementation-first approach leading to poor testability
Integration tests without proper mocking causing flaky behavior
Direct DOM manipulation bypassing React patterns
Monolithic implementation without separation of concerns
Breaking changes to existing interfaces during integration
